sections.plain_summary: Central sensitisation is a state in which the nervous system becomes amplified and over-reactive, so that normal or mild signals — pain, pressure, temperature, gut distension — are processed as more intense than they should be. It is not imaginary: measurable changes occur in how the spinal cord and brain process incoming signals. People with central sensitisation often experience widespread or disproportionate pain, hypersensitivity to touch, sound, light, or smell, and gut symptoms that appear to have no structural cause. It is a mechanism, not a single disease — and it underlies many chronic pain and functional conditions.
sections.detailed_summary:
Central sensitisation (CS) refers to a neuroplastic state in which the central nervous system — particularly the spinal cord dorsal horn and brain — undergoes functional and sometimes structural changes that lower the threshold for pain and other sensory signals. Once triggered, the system amplifies afferent input, reduces descending inhibition (the brain's natural 'turn-down' system), and can generate pain or discomfort without ongoing peripheral tissue damage.

In the gut, this same process is often called visceral hypersensitivity: the enteric and central nervous systems become sensitised to normal events like gas, stool movement, or distension, which are then interpreted as pain or urgency. Visceral hypersensitivity is considered a core mechanism in IBS and functional dyspepsia, but is also present in a proportion of people with IBD, endometriosis, and other conditions.

CS can be initiated by prolonged nociceptive input (e.g., unresolved injury or inflammation), psychological stress and trauma, infections, or dysfunction in the autonomic nervous system. Once established, it often persists even after the original trigger is removed, because the sensitisation itself has become self-sustaining through neuroplastic changes.

Key neurobiological features include: wind-up (progressive amplification of repeated stimuli), allodynia (pain from normally non-painful stimuli), hyperalgesia (exaggerated pain from mildly painful stimuli), and reduced conditioned pain modulation (the brain's ability to suppress pain using endogenous systems is impaired).

CS is increasingly recognised as a transdiagnostic mechanism linking fibromyalgia, IBS, chronic pelvic pain, migraines, temporomandibular disorder, chronic fatigue, and other overlapping syndromes. Self-directed investigation by patients is often more thorough than clinical assessment, because many clinicians still manage symptoms in isolation without considering the underlying sensitisation.

prevalence.summary: Central sensitisation is common in people with chronic pain or functional GI conditions, though population-level figures are difficult to measure because it is a mechanism rather than a formal diagnosis.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated to be present in 15–25% of chronic pain patients in some form; visceral hypersensitivity as a specific gut manifestation is found in approximately 60–70% of people with IBS in research settings
prevalence.notes: Population prevalence is contested because there is no single agreed diagnostic test for CS. Estimates vary widely depending on the condition studied and the criteria used. CS is more commonly identified in women and is frequently underdiagnosed in men. Some researchers argue CS-spectrum conditions affect upwards of 1 in 5 people with any chronic condition.

subgroups.name: Visceral hypersensitivity (gut-predominant)
subgroups.description: CS manifesting primarily through amplified gut sensory processing, producing pain, urgency, bloating, and nausea disproportionate to any structural finding.
distinguishing_features: Normal endoscopy and imaging despite significant symptoms
Pain or discomfort triggered by normal gut events (gas, distension, motility)
Strong overlap with IBS and functional dyspepsia
Rectal balloon distension testing may show lower pain thresholds in research settings

subgroups.name: Post-infectious central sensitisation
subgroups.description: CS that develops following an acute infection (viral, bacterial, or parasitic), sometimes called post-infectious IBS or post-COVID sensitisation.
distinguishing_features: Clear onset following an identifiable infection
Gut symptoms, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms may coexist
Inflammation may have resolved but sensitisation persists
Responds poorly to purely anti-inflammatory treatment

subgroups.name: Trauma-associated central sensitisation
subgroups.description: CS driven or maintained by psychological trauma, adverse childhood experiences, or PTSD, via dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and autonomic nervous system.
distinguishing_features: History of adverse childhood experiences or PTSD
Heightened baseline nervous system arousal
Symptoms often worse during stress or emotional triggers
May coexist with anxiety or depression
Can be significantly improved by trauma-informed therapies

subgroups.name: Widespread CS (central sensitisation syndrome)
subgroups.description: CS extending beyond a single body region or system to produce multi-site pain, fatigue, cognitive symptoms, and sensory sensitivities simultaneously.
distinguishing_features: Pain in multiple body regions without structural explanation
Sensitivity to light, sound, smell, and chemical exposures
Significant cognitive symptoms (brain fog, poor memory)
Strong overlap with fibromyalgia, ME/CFS, and MCAS
Central Sensitisation Inventory (CSI) score typically >40

subgroups.name: Hypermobility-associated CS
subgroups.description: CS in the context of hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) or hypermobility spectrum disorder, where connective tissue laxity generates persistent nociceptive input that drives sensitisation.
distinguishing_features: Joint hypermobility or Beighton score elevation
Dysautonomia (POTS) frequently co-occurring
Pain often widespread but with prominent joint and musculoskeletal features
Mast cell activation may also be present

symptoms.symptom: Abdominal pain or cramping from normal gut events (gas, distension, eating)
symptoms.notes: A hallmark of visceral hypersensitivity; pain is real but driven by amplified signalling, not structural damage

symptoms.symptom: Urgency or frequency of bowel movements without obvious cause
symptoms.notes: May alternate with constipation; gut motility can be altered by sensitised afferent signals

symptoms.symptom: Bloating and abdominal fullness disproportionate to food intake
symptoms.notes: Normal gas volumes may be perceived as severely painful or distending

symptoms.symptom: Nausea without structural or inflammatory explanation
symptoms.notes: Can be triggered by small meals, smells, or stress due to amplified gut-brain signalling
Often described as aching, burning, or flu-like; not always present in gut-predominant subtypes

symptoms.symptom: Allodynia — pain from light touch, clothing, or pressure
symptoms.notes: A specific marker of central sensitisation; normal touch signals are amplified to pain

symptoms.symptom: Hyperalgesia — exaggerated pain response to normally mildly painful stimuli
symptoms.notes: Distinct from allodynia; the pain threshold is not crossed by non-painful stimuli but the response to painful ones is disproportionate
Neurological amplification is metabolically costly; fatigue may be a direct consequence of sustained CS

symptoms.symptom: Cognitive difficulties (brain fog, poor concentration, word-finding problems)
symptoms.notes: Central sensitisation affects higher brain processing; correlated with pain intensity and sleep disruption
Poor sleep both results from and perpetuates CS — disrupted sleep impairs descending pain inhibition
A sign of generalised sensory amplification beyond the pain system
Often coexists with migraine in CS-spectrum presentations
Strong smells that most people tolerate may cause headaches, nausea, or malaise
Migraine is considered a CS-spectrum condition; it commonly co-occurs with other CS presentations
Visceral hypersensitivity in the pelvis can amplify menstrual pain and bladder discomfort even without endometriosis

symptoms.symptom: Bladder urgency or interstitial cystitis symptoms
symptoms.notes: The bladder is a visceral organ subject to the same sensitisation process as the gut
The same neurological changes that amplify pain also increase threat detection; anxiety both causes and results from CS

key_patterns: Symptoms that are disproportionate to any structural or inflammatory findings on imaging, endoscopy, or blood tests
Multiple body systems affected simultaneously (gut, musculoskeletal, neurological) without a unifying pathological diagnosis
Pain or discomfort triggered or worsened by stress, poor sleep, or sensory stimulation
History of a triggering event (infection, injury, trauma) followed by symptoms that outlast it
Coexistence of recognised CS-linked conditions: IBS, fibromyalgia, migraines, interstitial cystitis, temporomandibular disorder
Symptoms that fluctuate with nervous system state rather than with diet or structural factors alone
Central Sensitisation Inventory (CSI) score ≥40 out of 100

at_home.name: Central Sensitisation Inventory (CSI) self-report
at_home.description: A validated 25-item questionnaire measuring symptoms associated with central sensitisation syndrome. Scores ≥40 suggest CS is likely present. Freely available online. Useful as a starting point for self-understanding and for bringing to clinical appointments.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test. A high score supports the likelihood of CS but does not exclude other conditions.

at_home.name: Symptom tracking journal
at_home.description: Tracking symptoms alongside sleep quality, stress levels, menstrual cycle phase, and major life events (rather than only food intake) can reveal a nervous system pattern — where symptoms worsen with poor sleep or psychological stress regardless of diet. This pattern points toward CS rather than purely a dietary or structural cause.
at_home.safety_notes: Tracking can become anxiety-provoking for some people. If journalling increases symptom focus or distress, a lighter approach may be more useful.

at_home.name: Stress-symptom correlation observation
at_home.description: Deliberately noting whether symptom flares correlate with identifiable stressors, sleep deprivation, or emotional distress — rather than specific foods — can help distinguish CS from dietary intolerances. In CS, the nervous system state often predicts symptom severity more reliably than what was eaten.
at_home.safety_notes: This is observational only and cannot confirm or rule out concurrent dietary triggers.

clinical.name: Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST)
clinical.description: A battery of standardised tests measuring pain thresholds and pain tolerance to heat, cold, pressure, and vibration. Can objectively demonstrate allodynia, hyperalgesia, and impaired conditioned pain modulation. Most commonly used in research settings.
clinical.availability: Primarily available in academic pain centres and research hospitals. Rarely available in routine clinical practice. May be accessible via tertiary pain management referrals.

clinical.name: Central Sensitisation Inventory (CSI) — clinical administration
clinical.description: A validated 25-item clinician-administered or self-report questionnaire used widely in pain clinics to screen for CS. Score ≥40 is considered the clinical cut-off for probable CS.
clinical.availability: Can be administered in any clinical setting. Most commonly used by pain specialists, rheumatologists, and functional medicine practitioners.

clinical.name: Conditioned Pain Modulation (CPM) testing
clinical.description: Tests the brain's ability to suppress pain using endogenous descending inhibition. A cold pressor or pressure stimulus is applied while measuring pain response to a second stimulus. Impaired CPM is a measurable marker of CS. Predicts chronic pain outcomes.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist pain research centres and some tertiary pain clinics. Not routine in gastroenterology or general practice.

clinical.name: Rectal balloon distension testing
clinical.description: Used specifically to assess visceral hypersensitivity. A balloon is inflated in the rectum while the patient reports the first sensation, urge, and pain threshold. People with visceral hypersensitivity report pain at lower volumes than controls. Performed in motility or functional GI labs.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist gastroenterology and motility units. Primarily used in research and specialist functional GI evaluation.

clinical.name: Functional brain imaging (fMRI)
clinical.description: Research-level neuroimaging demonstrating altered brain activation patterns in response to stimuli in people with CS, confirming central rather than purely peripheral processing changes. Not used clinically for individual diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Research settings only. Not a routine clinical tool.

clinical.name: Comprehensive symptom and history review
clinical.description: In clinical practice, CS is most often identified through pattern recognition: multiple unexplained symptoms across body systems, disproportionate pain responses, normal structural investigations, and a compatible history. A skilled pain specialist or functional medicine doctor can diagnose CS clinically without specialised testing.
clinical.availability: Available to any practitioner trained in pain medicine, rheumatology, or functional medicine.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Visceral hypersensitivity — the gut manifestation of central sensitisation — is considered a core mechanism in IBS. A significant proportion of people with IBS show measurable lowered pain thresholds to gut distension compared to healthy controls.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Like IBS, functional dyspepsia involves sensitised gastric afferents and altered central processing of gut signals, producing pain, early satiety, and nausea without structural cause.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Fibromyalgia is widely considered the prototypical central sensitisation syndrome. Many people with fibromyalgia also have gut symptoms, and IBS and fibromyalgia co-occur at high rates — likely because both reflect the same underlying amplification mechanism.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: MCAS and CS frequently co-occur, particularly in the hEDS/POTS/MCAS triad. Mast cell mediators can sensitise peripheral nociceptors and contribute to gut inflammation that drives or maintains central sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / Hypermobility spectrum disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue laxity in hEDS generates persistent low-level nociceptive input from joints and soft tissues, which can drive and maintain central sensitisation over time. CS is extremely common in the hEDS population.

related_conditions.name: POTS / Dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic nervous system dysfunction and central sensitisation share overlapping neurobiology. Dysautonomia can amplify pain and gut symptoms through altered sympathetic regulation of visceral afferents.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic pelvic inflammation from endometriosis can drive central sensitisation over time. Even after surgical treatment of endometriosis, persistent CS may explain ongoing pelvic pain.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety and CS share overlapping neurobiology — both involve heightened threat detection and reduced inhibitory control. Anxiety can lower pain thresholds, and chronic pain can amplify anxiety, creating a bidirectional cycle.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Adverse childhood experiences and PTSD are among the most well-evidenced upstream drivers of CS. Trauma dysregulates the HPA axis and autonomic nervous system, priming the pain processing system for sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression impairs descending pain inhibition — the brain's ability to suppress pain signals from above. This directly worsens CS. Chronic pain simultaneously drives depression, creating a reinforcing cycle.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic floor dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Pelvic floor hypertonicity and CS frequently co-occur in people with chronic pelvic pain. Muscle guarding in response to pain can generate peripheral input that sustains central sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can generate ongoing gut inflammation and afferent nerve stimulation that, if prolonged, may contribute to visceral hypersensitivity and central sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine can sensitise peripheral nociceptors and is released by mast cells in the gut mucosa. In sensitised individuals, histamine load may lower the threshold for CS symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis may contribute to low-grade gut inflammation and altered gut-brain signalling, which can prime or sustain visceral hypersensitivity and central sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: Microscopic colitis
related_conditions.explanation: Microscopic colitis causes mucosal inflammation that is invisible on standard colonoscopy but can sensitise gut afferents. It may contribute to visceral hypersensitivity even when inflammation is controlled.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying generates prolonged gastric distension signals that can sensitise visceral afferents. CS and gastroparesis may coexist, complicating both diagnosis and management.

related_conditions.name: IBD in remission
related_conditions.explanation: A significant proportion of people with IBD in clinical remission continue to experience gut symptoms. Central sensitisation driven by prior inflammation is increasingly recognised as an explanation for persistent functional symptoms in IBD.

strategies.name: Pain neuroscience education (PNE)
strategies.description: Learning in detail about how central sensitisation works — that the nervous system has become amplified, that pain is real but not necessarily a signal of ongoing tissue damage — can itself reduce pain in many people. This is sometimes called 'explaining pain'. Understanding the mechanism reduces fear of pain, which directly reduces its amplification.
strategies.notes: Works best when delivered by a trained pain physiotherapist or psychologist and combined with other approaches. Reading Explain Pain (Moseley & Butler) is a widely recommended starting point.

strategies.name: Graded exposure and graded activity
strategies.description: Gradually and systematically increasing activity or exposure to triggers that have been avoided, in small steps that do not overwhelm the nervous system. Avoidance maintains and deepens sensitisation; gradual re-engagement teaches the nervous system that the world is safer than it currently believes.
strategies.notes: Must be genuinely gradual — too much too soon can cause setbacks. Best supervised by a pain physiotherapist experienced in CS. Distinct from 'push through the pain' advice, which is counterproductive.

strategies.name: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) for chronic pain
strategies.description: CBT adapted for chronic pain addresses catastrophising, pain-related fear, and unhelpful coping patterns that perpetuate sensitisation. Strong evidence base for reducing pain intensity, disability, and distress.
strategies.notes: Most effective when the therapist has specific chronic pain training. Generic CBT for anxiety or depression alone may not address the pain-specific cognitions involved.

strategies.name: Trauma-informed therapy (EMDR, somatic therapies, IFS)
strategies.description: For people with CS driven by unresolved trauma, therapies that process traumatic memory and reduce autonomic dysregulation — such as EMDR, somatic experiencing, or Internal Family Systems — may address a root driver of sensitisation rather than managing symptoms alone.
strategies.notes: Evidence is growing but most studies are in PTSD populations. Clinical experience in CS/fibromyalgia populations is promising. Finding a trauma-informed therapist with understanding of somatic presentations is important.

strategies.name: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN)
strategies.description: At very low doses (1.5–4.5 mg/day), naltrexone appears to modulate microglial activity and reduce neuroinflammation, which may underlie CS in some people. Used off-label for fibromyalgia, IBS, and chronic pain. Some patients report significant symptom reduction.
strategies.notes: Off-label use; requires a prescribing physician willing to consider it. Evidence base is growing but not yet large. Generally well tolerated. Works best in people where neuroinflammation is a driver.

strategies.name: Sleep restoration
strategies.description: Poor sleep directly impairs descending pain inhibition — the brain's ability to suppress pain signals. Restoring sleep quality, through any combination of sleep hygiene, CBT-I, or medication if needed, can meaningfully reduce CS symptom severity.
strategies.notes: Sleep is often underweighted in CS management. Even partial improvements in sleep quality can produce measurable reductions in pain and gut symptoms.

strategies.name: Aerobic exercise (gradually introduced)
strategies.description: Regular moderate aerobic exercise activates endogenous opioid and cannabinoid systems and stimulates descending pain inhibition. Exercise-induced hypoalgesia has strong evidence in CS populations. Starting very gently and building slowly is essential.
strategies.notes: Post-exertional malaise in some CS/ME/CFS overlap presentations means high-intensity exercise can worsen symptoms. Heart rate monitoring and staying in low-intensity zones may be important for this subgroup.

strategies.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
strategies.description: Structured mindfulness practice reduces activity in pain-amplifying brain regions and can improve conditioned pain modulation. The 8-week MBSR programme has a reasonable evidence base in fibromyalgia and chronic pain.
strategies.notes: Effects are moderate rather than dramatic for most people. Works best as part of a broader approach. Some people with trauma find standard mindfulness practices dysregulating — trauma-adapted versions may be more appropriate.

strategies.name: Dietary strategies for visceral hypersensitivity
strategies.description: When CS manifests primarily as visceral hypersensitivity, low-FODMAP eating, reducing gut fermentation, and avoiding known irritants (alcohol, caffeine, highly processed foods) can reduce peripheral gut stimulation and therefore reduce afferent input that sustains sensitisation. Diet alone rarely resolves CS, but reducing peripheral input can lower overall symptom burden.
strategies.notes: FODMAP restriction is a symptom management tool, not a cure. Long-term restriction should be guided by a FODMAP-trained registered dietitian to avoid nutritional adequacy issues and unnecessary elimination.

strategies.name: Tricyclic antidepressants and SNRIs (neuromodulators)
strategies.description: Low-dose tricyclics (e.g., amitriptyline, nortriptyline) and SNRIs (e.g., duloxetine, venlafaxine) modulate central pain processing pathways independently of their antidepressant effects. They enhance descending inhibition and can meaningfully reduce CS symptom burden, including in visceral hypersensitivity.
strategies.notes: These are neurological modulators at the doses used, not primarily antidepressants. Some patients resist them due to stigma about 'antidepressants'. Discussing the neuromodulatory mechanism with a prescriber can be clarifying.

strategies.name: Vagus nerve stimulation and breathing practices
strategies.description: Slow diaphragmatic breathing, extended exhalation, and other vagal tone practices activate the parasympathetic nervous system and reduce sympathetic dominance that perpetuates CS. Non-invasive vagal stimulation devices are also emerging.
strategies.notes: Low-cost, low-risk, and immediately accessible. Most useful as a daily practice rather than an acute intervention.

things_to_avoid: Excessive reassurance-seeking and investigation cycles — repeated normal test results without a framework for understanding them can increase anxiety rather than reduce it, which perpetuates CS
Complete rest and activity avoidance — while rest is appropriate acutely, prolonged avoidance maintains and deepens sensitisation
High-intensity exercise before the nervous system is ready — exercising through significant pain flares, particularly in ME/CFS overlap presentations, can worsen post-exertional symptoms
Framing symptoms as purely psychological — invalidating neurological changes drives shame, delayed help-seeking, and missed downstream diagnoses
Framing symptoms as purely structural — continuing to search only for tissue damage prevents engagement with the neurological mechanisms that are actually driving symptoms
Long-term opioid use for CS-driven pain — opioids can paradoxically worsen central sensitisation over time through opioid-induced hyperalgesia
Dismissing the psychological component entirely — nervous system regulation requires addressing the whole system, including emotional and cognitive inputs

Drastic dietary elimination without professional guidance — while reducing peripheral gut stimulation helps, extreme restriction adds stress, malnutrition risk, and food-related anxiety that can worsen CS

uncomfortable_truths.truth: No amount of further investigation will find the 'cause' that, once removed, makes everything normal again — because the sensitisation is now in the nervous system itself, not in a damaged organ waiting to be found.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: The medical culture most people have grown up in frames illness as 'something wrong with a part', and resolution as 'finding and fixing it'. Accepting that the nervous system has reorganised itself feels more threatening and less tractable than a structural diagnosis.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who accept this shift their energy from investigation to nervous system rehabilitation. Many achieve substantial, measurable reduction in symptom burden within 6–18 months by working with pain neuroscience education, graded activity, sleep, and psychological approaches in combination.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Trauma and psychological state are often significant drivers of central sensitisation — and addressing them is part of the biology, not a concession that symptoms are imaginary.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told psychological factors are involved feels like being told the pain is not real, or is the patient's fault. Many people have fought hard to be taken seriously as physically unwell, and accepting a role for psychological drivers can feel like capitulating to dismissive clinicians.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who frame trauma-informed and psychological work as neurological rehabilitation — not as 'it's all in your head' — often achieve significant pain reduction through EMDR, somatic therapies, or CBT that purely biomedical approaches have not produced.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Diet changes alone rarely resolve central sensitisation, even when they help with symptoms.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Dietary changes are controllable, tangible, and feel like active problem-solving. They also sometimes produce genuine short-term relief, which can be mistaken for addressing the underlying mechanism.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who use diet as one tool within a broader nervous system approach — while also addressing sleep, activity, stress, and neurological drivers — tend to get further than those who cycle through increasingly restrictive dietary regimes without improvement in baseline function.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Symptom flares are very often predicted by nervous system state — sleep, stress, hormonal shifts — more than by specific exposures. This means individual tolerance is not fixed and will vary, which makes simple trigger avoidance an incomplete strategy.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Variable reactivity is deeply confusing. People naturally look for a consistent rule ('if I avoid X I'll be fine'). Acknowledging that the nervous system state itself determines tolerance challenges the search for a simple dietary or environmental answer.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Accepting variability allows people to shift from trying to avoid all triggers to building a nervous system that is less reactive overall. Lower baseline sensitisation means higher tolerance across the board, including to foods and stimuli that previously caused problems.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Understanding that the nervous system has amplified — not that tissue is damaged — directly reduces pain catastrophising and fear-avoidance behaviour, both of which maintain sensitisation. Knowledge of the mechanism is itself therapeutic.
key_levers.how_to_start: Read Explain Pain by Lorimer Moseley and David Butler, or watch Moseley's TED-style lectures online. Consider one session with a pain physiotherapist to discuss the model.
key_levers.timeline: Shifts in understanding can begin within days to weeks; observable pain reduction often follows over 1–3 months as behavioural changes follow

key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep is the most powerful modulator of descending pain inhibition. Chronic poor sleep creates a neurobiological state that makes CS worse by definition. Improving sleep is one of the highest-leverage interventions available.
key_levers.how_to_start: Start with sleep hygiene basics (consistent schedule, dark/cool room, no screens 1 hour before bed). If this is insufficient, seek CBT for insomnia (CBT-I) — more effective than medication long-term.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep improvements often begin within 2–4 weeks; pain and gut symptom reduction typically follows within 1–3 months of consistently improved sleep

key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise activates endogenous pain inhibition systems that CS has impaired. Regular aerobic movement is one of the few interventions that can measurably restore conditioned pain modulation over time.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with 10–15 minutes of low-intensity walking on alternate days. Increase by no more than 10% per week. Track symptom response to find a sustainable starting level.
key_levers.timeline: 4–12 weeks to begin noticing reduced baseline pain; 3–6 months of consistent practice for meaningful functional improvement

key_levers.lever: Addressing upstream trauma or psychological drivers
key_levers.why_it_matters: For people with trauma-associated CS, the sensitisation is being maintained by unresolved nervous system dysregulation. Symptom management alone treats the output without changing the input. Processing trauma can produce durable changes in baseline sensitivity.
key_levers.how_to_start: Seek a therapist trained in somatic or trauma-informed approaches (EMDR, somatic experiencing, or trauma-focused CBT). Look specifically for practitioners familiar with chronic pain or medically unexplained symptoms.
key_levers.timeline: Variable — some people notice meaningful changes within 8–16 sessions; others require longer-term work

key_levers.why_it_matters: Low-dose tricyclics, SNRIs, or low-dose naltrexone can recalibrate central pain processing, improve sleep, and reduce baseline sensitisation. For some people this creates a window of reduced symptoms in which other rehabilitation is possible.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss neuromodulator options (amitriptyline, duloxetine, or LDN) with a GP, pain specialist, or functional medicine doctor who understands CS. Frame the conversation around neuromodulation, not antidepressants.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks to assess initial response to tricyclics or SNRIs; LDN effects may take 8–12 weeks

key_levers.why_it_matters: CS is maintained by total allostatic load — the cumulative demand on the nervous system. Reducing inputs from multiple sources (poor sleep, overwork, relational stress, dietary irritants, sensory overload) reduces the overall burden that sustains sensitisation.
key_levers.how_to_start: Map all current nervous system stressors across sleep, social, work, dietary, and sensory domains. Identify two or three modifiable factors to address first, rather than trying to fix everything at once.
key_levers.timeline: Ongoing lifestyle recalibration; meaningful symptom improvement often noticed within 3–6 months of consistent load reduction

common_mistakes: Continuing to pursue structural diagnoses rather than engaging with neurological rehabilitation — investigation loops consume time and energy without changing the underlying sensitisation
Starting exercise too intensely — overdoing activity early causes flares that reinforce pain-avoidance behaviour and set back rehabilitation
Treating sleep as optional — most other interventions underperform when sleep deprivation is ongoing
Using opioids for long-term CS pain management — may provide short-term relief but likely worsens sensitisation over time
Expecting a single intervention to resolve CS — it is a system-level problem requiring a system-level approach across multiple domains simultaneously

Restricting diet further and further in search of triggers while ignoring nervous system state — dietary changes may reduce symptom intensity marginally but cannot resolve the underlying amplification
Avoiding all pain and discomfort — graded exposure is essential; complete avoidance maintains the nervous system in a threat-detection state
Waiting for a 'cure' before engaging with rehabilitation — the rehabilitation process is itself the treatment, not a bridge to something else

specialists.role: Delivers pain neuroscience education, supervises graded activity and exposure, and addresses movement-related fear-avoidance. Often the single most useful starting practitioner for CS.
specialists.notes: Look specifically for physiotherapists trained in pain neuroscience education (PNE) or with experience in complex chronic pain. A standard physiotherapist treating symptoms locally may not have this training.

specialists.type: Pain psychologist or psychologist trained in chronic pain CBT
specialists.role: Addresses catastrophising, pain-related fear, sleep, and psychological perpetuating factors. Works alongside medical and physical approaches.
specialists.notes: Specifically seek a psychologist with chronic pain specialism. Generic CBT or counselling practitioners may not have the relevant training.

specialists.role: May investigate upstream drivers of CS including gut inflammation, hormonal dysregulation, nutrient deficiencies affecting neurological function, and autonomic nervous system state. More likely to take a root-cause approach than a symptom-management approach.
specialists.notes: Mainstream clinicians often manage individual symptoms in isolation without a CS framework. Functional medicine practitioners are more likely to consider the whole picture, though quality varies significantly.

specialists.role: Provides coordinated access to pain medicine, physiotherapy, and psychology together. The most evidence-based format for complex CS, offering intensive interdisciplinary pain rehabilitation programmes.
specialists.notes: Waiting lists can be long. These clinics vary in their adoption of modern pain neuroscience approaches — some still lean toward procedural pain management.

specialists.role: For visceral hypersensitivity presentations, a gastroenterologist specialising in functional disorders (rather than organic disease) can diagnose and manage gut-specific CS, offer rectal distension testing, and prescribe neuromodulators for gut symptom reduction.
specialists.notes: Many gastroenterologists focus primarily on ruling out structural disease. Specifically seeking one with an interest in functional GI disorders and visceral hypersensitivity will produce more useful consultations.

specialists.role: For CS with significant trauma history, addresses the root nervous system dysregulation driving sensitisation. Works below the level of cognitive talk therapy through body-based and memory-reprocessing approaches.
specialists.notes: Increasingly available but still specialist. The Trauma Therapist directory and EMDR Association websites can help locate qualified practitioners.

specialists.role: Can diagnose fibromyalgia and CS syndrome, administer CSI assessment, exclude inflammatory conditions, and initiate neuromodulatory medication.
specialists.notes: Many rheumatologists primarily focus on inflammatory conditions and may offer limited support for non-inflammatory CS. Seeking one with explicit fibromyalgia or central sensitisation syndrome experience is worthwhile.

patient_communities.name: r/ChronicPain
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community for people with chronic pain of all types. Useful for peer support, sharing practitioner experiences, and learning how others navigate CS-related challenges. Quality of advice varies — apply critical thinking.

patient_communities.name: r/Fibromyalgia
patient_communities.notes: Fibromyalgia is the prototypical CS syndrome. This community discusses CS mechanisms, treatments, and lived experience in depth. Highly relevant even for people without a formal fibromyalgia diagnosis.

patient_communities.name: Pain Revolution community
patient_communities.notes: Built around the pain neuroscience education movement from Lorimer Moseley's group. Includes educational resources, practitioner directories, and community. Excellent starting point for understanding CS.

patient_communities.name: The IBS Network
patient_communities.notes: For people with visceral hypersensitivity presenting as IBS. Includes self-management resources and a peer support forum. Relevant because IBS and gut-predominant CS overlap substantially.

patient_communities.name: Curable app community
patient_communities.notes: A chronic pain self-management app built on pain neuroscience education principles, with an active community of users. Particularly useful for CS rehabilitation outside clinical settings.

learning_resources.title: Explain Pain
learning_resources.description: The foundational accessible text on pain neuroscience education. Written for patients and clinicians alike, it explains in clear terms what central sensitisation is, why pain is always real, and what can be done. Reading this book is considered by many pain specialists to be therapeutic in itself.

learning_resources.title: The Explain Pain Handbook: Protectometer
learning_resources.description: A practical workbook companion to Explain Pain that helps individuals identify and address their specific danger signals and safety signals using the Protectometer tool. Useful for active self-rehabilitation.

learning_resources.title: Why Do I Hurt? (TEDx-style lecture)
learning_resources.description: A widely shared, engaging lecture by leading pain neuroscience researcher Lorimer Moseley explaining the mechanisms of chronic pain and central sensitisation. An excellent accessible starting point.

learning_resources.title: Central Sensitization Inventory (CSI): Established as a Valid and Reliable Tool
learning_resources.description: The original validation study for the Central Sensitisation Inventory. Relevant for those who want to understand the scientific basis of CS assessment and the evidence for CS as a measurable construct.

learning_resources.title: Pain Revolution
learning_resources.description: Educational website providing free pain neuroscience resources, practitioner directories, and community access. Excellent ongoing resource for people learning about CS.

learning_resources.title: Unlearn Your Pain
learning_resources.description: A self-directed programme for chronic pain based on the mind-body / neural pathway sensitisation model. Particularly useful for people where psychological and emotional drivers are prominent in their CS presentation. Includes structured exercises.

learning_resources.title: Gut Feelings: The Intelligence of the Unconscious
learning_resources.description: While not a clinical text, this book explores the science of interoception and gut-brain communication in accessible terms. Useful context for understanding why the gut is so profoundly influenced by brain state.

learning_resources.title: The Sensitive Nervous System
learning_resources.description: A more detailed clinical text on neurodynamics and central sensitisation, written primarily for physiotherapists but accessible to motivated patients. Goes deeper into mechanisms than Explain Pain.

learning_resources.title: Curable
learning_resources.description: A structured app and programme for chronic pain rehabilitation built on pain neuroscience education principles. Provides guided audio programmes, journalling tools, and a community. Useful for people without immediate access to a pain psychologist or physiotherapist.

sections.relevance_signal: **Pain disproportionate to physical findings**. Normal endoscopy but significant gut pain. Symptoms also in other body areas — widespread pain sensitivity, fibromyalgia overlap.
